preFix.title: Fitting a mirror to a tiled wall
preFix.subtitle: Drill through ceramic tiles and fix a mirror or bathroom cabinet to a tiled wall without cracking the tiles
preFix.excerpt: How to drill through wall tiles and fix a mirror or cabinet to the tiled wall behind. Covers the correct drill bit, drilling technique, fixing choice for masonry or plasterboard behind the tiles, and positioning the mirror level.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional domestic tiling practice.

content.text: The risk when drilling tiles is cracking them, usually from drilling too fast, using the wrong bit, or engaging the hammer action. Work through this method and the tile drills cleanly.

Position fixings in the field of a tile, not in the grout joint. Grout joints over timber studs in a cavity wall are often cracked already; a fixing through a grout line is under-supported. Mark the fixing positions with the spirit level and masking tape, writing on masking tape with a pencil is easier to clean off afterwards than marks on a glazed tile.
Step 2, Check what is behind the tiles
Tap the wall. A solid thud means brick or block behind the tiles, standard masonry plugs work. A hollow sound means plasterboard, use

rated for the weight of the mirror or cabinet. Check with a cable-and-pipe detector before drilling: hot and cold water supply pipes run vertically and horizontally from bath or basin taps, and are often within 150 mm of the surface.

of the correct diameter (match to the wall plug). Start the drill at the centre of the mark with the speed on its lowest setting and with NO hammer action, the impact shatters glaze on ceramic or cracks porcelain. Keep steady, light pressure. Place a strip of masking tape over the mark first; it prevents the bit from skating across the glazed tile surface before it bites. Once through the tile and into the adhesive bed, swap to a standard masonry bit at the same diameter to complete the depth into the wall.
Step 4, Insert the plug and fix

Insert the wall plug so its head is flush with the tile surface. For cavity walls behind tiles, use a hollow-wall anchor with a collar designed for tile thickness (these are sold specifically as bathroom or tile fixings). Screw the mirror or bracket firmly without over-torquing, ceramic tiles crack under the compression of an overtightened screw. Tighten to snug-firm; a short screwdriver gives more feel than a power driver here.

Adhesive mirror mounting: for a lightweight flat mirror (under 5 kg), mirror adhesive applied to the back of the mirror in blobs (never full coverage, moisture must be able to escape) is a drilling-free alternative. Requires a clean, dry tile surface.
Porcelain tiles: harder to drill than ceramic. Use a diamond-core tile bit at very slow speed with constant water cooling (a ring of plumber's putty around the drill point, filled with water).

items.fix: The tile is cracked and must be replaced. Drill future tiles at low speed with no hammer action.
items.cause: Hammer action engaged, or drilling speed too high

items.fix: Apply masking tape to the mark, or nick a small central point in the glaze with the corner of a cold chisel and a light tap before drilling.
items.cause: No masking tape or guide point to locate the bit
items.symptom: Drill bit skates across the tile surface

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Anchor" — generic equivalent is "salted butter"
paragraph reads at grade 11.4 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
